findings.description: assemble() iterates range(total_chunks - 1), silently dropping the final chunk so every reassembled file is truncated and corrupted despite passing the completeness check.
session.filename flows unsanitized into os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename), so '../' or an absolute path escapes UPLOAD_DIR enabling arbitrary file write (path traversal / potential RCE).
upload_id is interpolated into the chunk filename without validation, allowing '/' or '..' to redirect chunk read/write/delete outside CHUNK_DIR (path traversal).
receive_chunk enforces no per-chunk or aggregate size limit, so MAX_FILE_MB is never honored during upload, permitting unbounded disk consumption (DoS).
verify_checksum loads the entire file into memory with f.read() instead of streaming, so a 200MB-allowed file exhausts RAM under concurrency (DoS).
self._sessions dict and session.received set are mutated across start/receive_chunk/assemble/abort with no locking, racing under concurrent (typical multi-threaded) request handling.
del self._sessions[upload_id] raises KeyError if abort() or a concurrent assemble() already removed the session, unlike abort()'s safe .pop(..., None).

File I/O in assemble (and receive_chunk/verify_checksum) has no exception handling; a missing chunk file raises FileNotFoundError mid-write, leaving a partial file in UPLOAD_DIR and orphaned chunks with cleanup never reached.

assemble() stores and returns the file without ever invoking verify_checksum or file_size_ok, so corrupted/oversized uploads are persisted and the verify/size guarantees are never enforced in the flow.
start() overwrites an existing in-flight session for the same upload_id, discarding its received set and orphaning already-written chunk files on disk.
Destination write is non-atomic and collision-prone: same filename overwrites prior uploads and a partially written file is visible in UPLOAD_DIR (no temp-file + rename).
Extension allowlist performs no content/MIME validation, so a polyglot or HTML payload disguised as .png/.pdf passes and could enable stored XSS or content-type confusion when served.
makedirs creates UPLOAD_DIR/CHUNK_DIR with default umask permissions and no explicit mode, risking world-readable storage of uploaded files.
makedirs in __init__ raises hard (e.g., permission/parent-missing) coupling object construction to filesystem availability with no graceful handling.
